For example, if it’s a 7945HX, the CPU is a part of the Ryzen 9 series.

Third digit

Here’s where it gets interesting. The third digit shows the AMD processor’s microarchitecture. The company uses the Zen term, which indicates the fabrication process in nanometers. For example, if the third digit is 3, the CPU is based on an older Zen 3/Zen 3+ 7nm/6nm microarchitecture. The latest Zen 4 CPUs have 4 as the third digit, and they are based on AMD’s more advanced 5nm fabrication process.

Fourth digit

The last digit further separates different processors within the segment. Let’s take 7730U and 7735U as an example here. Both are part of the Ryzen 7 segment. However, the former is a 15W CPU with a 2GHz base clock and up to 4.5GHz max boost clock speeds. The latter is a 28W CPU with a 2.7GHz base and up to 4.75GHz max boost clock speeds. There is also a difference in graphics core count (8 for 7730U vs. 12 for 7735U).

Suffix and its meaning

Source: XDA Developers

Finally, the last letter in an AMD mobile CPU is TDP (Thermal Design Power) in watts, which refers to power consumption under maximum load. Here’s what each letter means.

  • E (9W): It’s basically a fanless variant for U-series CPUs and is used in affordable laptops.
  • C (15-28W): Designed for power-efficient Chromebook devices.
  • U (15-28W): Lower-clocked laptop processors for thin and light devices.
  • HS (35W+): High-performance CPUs with integrated graphics and lesser power draw.
  • HX (55W+): Delivers maximum performance in AMD’s mobile processor lineup.

Decoding AMD Ryzen desktop processors

Interestingly, AMD hasn’t yet rolled out a new naming scheme for desktop chips like it did for laptops. For example, the 7000-series of desktop processors, such as 7950X, 7900X, 7700X and more, are based on Zen 4 architecture and don’t really follow the same naming scheme as discussed above.

Similarly, the Ryzen 9000 desktop processors, which are slated to release on July 31, don't use any such naming scheme. Team Red recently announced Zen 5-based Ryzen 9 9950X, Ryzen 9 9900X, Ryzen 7 9700X, and Ryzen 5 9600X CPUs.

Source: AMD

The first digit is a model number that indicates the processor's series. For instance, the Ryzen 7xxx series is a successor to the Ryzen 5xxx series and offers the newest microarchitecture available today from AMD.

The second number displays the performance level within its series. It basically indicates a minor jump in clock speeds. For example, AMD Ryzen 5 5600GT is better than Ryzen 5 5500GT (up to 4.6GHz vs. up to 4.4GHz max boost clock).

The third and fourth digits show further differentiation within the same segment. Let’s take the Ryzen 9 7940HX and 7945HX as an example. The latter is marginally better due to a higher max boost clock (up to 5.4GHz vs. 5.2GHz) and base clock speeds (2.5GHz vs. 2.4GHz).

AMD Ryzen desktop CPU suffixes and what they mean

Like its mobile processor lineup, the desktop CPUs also come with different suffixes at the end.

  • F: Lacks integrated graphics.
  • G: APU with high-performance AMD Ryzen integrated graphics.
  • GE: Has integrated AMD Radeon graphics but lower performance.
  • X: Has faster clock speeds and higher power consumption.
  • XT: Marginally better than X due to higher clock speeds.
  • X3D: Comes with AMD’s 3D V-Cache technology for better gaming performance.
  • S: Prioritizes efficiency and comes with integrated AMD Radeon Vega graphics.

Simplify AMD’s new naming scheme

The CPU is the heart of any system. Picking up the wrong AMD CPU can ruin your workstation experience in no time. You should understand AMD’s naming scheme before hunting for a new laptop or a desktop unit. Once you purchase a desktop AMD processor, read our step-by-step guide to install it properly.

item.title: Best accessories for Raspberry Pi 5
item.itunes:title: Best accessories for Raspberry Pi 5
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The Raspberry Pi 5 is the latest entry in the uber-popular SBC lineup, and it packs enough upgrades to make it a worthwhile addition to any tinkerer's workshop. From its superior quad-core Arm Cortex A76 processor to the new PCIe Gen 2 interface and a dedicated power button, this powerful little SBC is not only great for hobbyist projects, but can also serve as a portable PC for everyday tasks.

Although you can technically use it in a headless setup, you might want to look into some extra peripherals if you want to push this palm-sized system to its limit. With so many Raspberry Pi accessories available online, it's easy to feel overwhelmed. But don't worry, we’ve curated a list of the best peripherals to help you make the most out of your Raspberry Pi.

Related
Raspberry Pi 5 review: The holy grail of DIY projects got even better (and rarer)

The Raspberry Pi 5 is one of the most powerful consumer-grade SBCs out there. Sadly, its limited stock means you'll have a hard time finding one.

Our top picks for Raspberry Pi 5 accessories in 2024

  • SanDisk Ultra microSD card
    $22 $26 Save $4

    SanDisk has plenty of microSD cards to choose from but we think the Ultras are among the best when it comes to affordable options. These SD cards still offer read speeds of up to 100MB/s and come in multiple size options, although 128GB is a great capacity to go with.

    $22 at Amazon
  • Best camera module
    Freenove 5MP Camera for Raspberry Pi

    The newest member of the Raspberry Pi family supports dual 4-lane MIPI connectors instead of separate DSI and CSI ports. As such, it requires a specialized 15-pin ribbon connector instead of the usual 22-pin cable. This kit from Freenove contains both cables alongside a 5MP camera module that you can mount on an adjustable holder.

    $12 at Amazon
  • Best touchscreen display
    GeeekPi 7 Inch Capacitive Touchscreen for Raspberry Pi

    GeeekPi’s 7-inch touchscreen display has a 1024x600 resolution and a 60Hz refresh rate, which is more than enough for most Raspberry Pi 5 projects. Besides providing audio output thanks to a set of speakers, the portable monitor can also be mounted on top of the latest Raspberry Pi board, making it perfect for those who want a compact display for the SBC.

    $60 at Amazon
  • Best metal case
    Geekworm Metal Case P579-V2

    Since the Raspberry Pi 5 doesn’t ship with a protective case, it can easily accumulate dust or get damaged by accidental spillage. As such, the Geekworm P579-V2 Metal Case is the perfect way to safeguard your Raspberry Pi 5. Plus, it’s a large case that provides ample space for you to plug in other accessories. What’s more, the P579-V2 also includes the official Active Cooler, so you don’t have to buy the latter separately.
     

    $23 at Amazon
  • Best cooler
    Raspberry Pi Active Cooler

    Aside from the low supply, one of the biggest issues you’ll encounter while using the Raspberry Pi 5 is its tendency to overheat at even the most basic workloads. Therefore, it’s a good idea to invest in the Official Active Cooler for the Raspberry Pi 5 as it not only includes a highly-capable blower fan, but also has an aluminum heatsink and pre-applied thermal pads to prevent the SBC from getting too toasty.

    $13 at Amazon
  • Best USB Hub
    Anker 4-Port USB 3.0 Hub
    $15 $18 Save $3

    If you’re someone who loves to make projects with the Raspberry Pi, it’s possible to run out of ports on the device after plugging in all your peripherals. That’s where Anker’s inexpensive USB comes in handy: by splitting one of the Raspberry Pi 5’s USB 3.0 sockets into four ports, you can easily connect all your USB-powered accessories to the SBC.
     

    $15 at Amazon
  • GeeekPi N04 M.2 NVMe to PCIe Adapter
    $15 $23 Save $8

    Up until the Raspberry Pi 4, you could only connect portable SSDs to the Raspberry Pi boards, but thanks to the PCIe interface, you can even pair a high-speed NVMe SSD with the SBC. Since you can’t directly plug the SSD into the PCIe interface, you’ll need an NVMe to PCIe adapter, and the GeeekPi N04 HAT is one of the best HATs for this purpose.
     

    $15 at Amazon
  • Best power supply
    CanaKit 45W USB-C Power Supply

    Unlike the Raspberry Pi 4, which requires a power supply of 5V/3A, the Raspberry Pi Foundation raised the power requirements of the newest RPi board to 5V/5A. While you can use any ol’ power adapter with the Raspberry Pi 5, doing so will limit the amount of current sent to the USB ports. This makes CanaKit’s 45W power adapter perfect for the SBC, and its high power rating means you can use it to charge other devices, too.
     

    $20 at Amazon
  • Ugreen USB Sound Card
    $14 $17 Save $3

    With the AUX port missing from the Raspberry Pi 5, you’ll require some kind of adapter to play back audio from the SBC. Ugreen's USB Sound Card is perfect for this purpose; unlike your average USB-to-AUX adapter, this sound card has a built-in DAC to improve sound quality. To top it off, it even has a microphone port that you can use for audio recording purposes.
     

    $14 at Amazon
  • Best SSD
    Kingston NV2 2280 NVMe SSD
    $61 $81 Save $20

    While you can technically pair ultra-fast PCIe Gen 5 SSDs to the Raspberry Pi 5, you’re not going to see much of a difference if you opt for a cheaper NVMe drive. Kingston is well-known for manufacturing high-quality drives that strike the perfect balance between price and performance, and the company’s NV2 M.2 2280 is easily one of the best SSDs for the flagship Raspberry Pi board.

    $61 at Amazon
  • GeeekPi Mini Tower Kit

    If you’re planning to use your Raspberry Pi 5 as a miniature PC, then you might want to consider the GeeekPi Mini Tower Kit. Aside from the RGB-lit mini tower case, you also get a GPIO extension board alongside a powerful air cooler. As if that’s not enough, the case even houses a small OLED screen, which you can freely program with the Raspberry Pi 5.

    $36 at Amazon
  • Best Micro HDMI Cable
    Amazon Basics Micro HDMI to HDMI Cable

    Modern monitors and graphics cards are equipped with full-sized HDMI or DisplayPort interfaces. So, the average user may not possess the adapters and cables for smaller HDMI ports. Unfortunately, the Raspberry Pi 5 continues the trend of sending video output via micro HDMI. This makes the Amazon Basics Micro HDMI to HDMI Display Cable an essential accessory for anyone who has to connect the Raspberry Pi 5 to a monitor.

    $11 at Amazon

Enhance your Raspberry Pi 5's functionality with our favorite peripherals

While there’s no doubt each of the accessories above can improve your Raspberry Pi 5 experience, you don’t have to go out and buy them all at once. Apart from the microSD card and the Micro HDMI to HDMI cable, the rest of the peripherals, cables, and cases depend entirely on your use-case.

For newcomers, I’d suggest buying the Geekworm P579-V2 Metal Case, as it not only provides solid protection to your Raspberry Pi 5 but also prevents it from hitting obscenely high temperatures thanks to its active cooling. From there, consider picking up the Anker 4-Port USB 3.0 Hub and GeeekPi Touchscreen Display, as they are useful in almost any project.

Related
Channel your inner DIY god by building these 10 complex Raspberry Pi projects

Here are ten complex Raspberry Pi projects to train your tinkering muscles

Conversely, you can probably save the Freenove camera module for last since it's a niche product with specific uses-cases, and the same holds true for the PCIe SSD and its adapter. But if you're not willing to drop hundreds of dollars on optional peripherals, there are a number of cool ideas you can bring to life with your Raspberry Pi.

Raspberry Pi 5

The Raspberry Pi is back, and the fifth iteration of the SBC is a lot more capable than the older models. From a new quad-core Arm Cortex-A76 CPU, support for dual monitor setups at 4K 60Hz, and a dedicated power button, there's a lot to love about this palm-sized computer.

$60 at Adafruit $60 at CanaKit

item.title: How to use the PS5 DualSense controller on a Windows PC
item.itunes:title: How to use the PS5 DualSense controller on a Windows PC
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Quick Links

  • Connecting the PS5 controller to your PC
  • How to set up the DualSense controller with Steam
  • Using the DualSense controller with non-Steam games
  • What Steam games support the DualSense controller?

When you think of PC gaming, you probably think of a mouse and keyboard. That makes sense considering you already use a mouse and keyboard for everything else and they're incredibly versatile and precise. But some games are just best played with a controller, and if you happen to own a PlayStation 5, you can use the DualSense controller with your Windows 11 (or 10) PC.

The DualSense has a significantly different design from its predecessors. The Xbox Wireless Controller is still arguably the best PC controller on the market, but the DualSense is certainly good enough that if you have a PlayStation 5 already, you can avoid buying a new controller and just stick with the controller that came with your console. Here's how you can set it up.

Related
How to optimize Windows 11 for gaming

PC gamers on Windows 11, take note

Connecting the PS5 controller to your PC

Wired connection

There are two ways to connect the DualSense controller to your PC. The easiest is to simply plug in using the USB Type-C port on the controller and a USB port on your PC. You just need a USB cable, which you probably already have, whether it's from your phone or another device. The cable does need to support data throughput, which some cables don't so if you try one and it doesn't work, try a different cable before you give up.

Bluetooth (wireless) connection

If you want to go wireless, the PlayStation 5 controller also supports Bluetooth so that you can connect that way. Most modern laptops and PCs have Bluetooth these days, though you may need an adapter for older machines. Connecting a controller this way is still fairly straightforward. Here's how it goes:

  1. Open the Settings app on your PC and go to the Bluetooth & devices section (in Windows 11) or Devices (in Windows 10).
  2. Click Add device at the top of the page.
  3. Choose Bluetooth in the window that pops up to start searching for devices.
  4. Enter pairing mode on the PlayStation 5 controller by holding the PlayStation and Share buttons at the same time until the lights around the touchpad turn on.
  5. On your PC, the controller should appear as a Wireless controller. Select it and wait for the connection to finish.

Now your controller is connected to your PC, but the experience isn't exactly seamless from here. Windows is designed to specifically work with one kind of input based on the Xbox Wireless Controller, so that's what most games support out of the box. Thankfully, there are solutions for this.

How to set up the DualSense controller with Steam

Steam is the most popular gaming platform on PC, and one of the great things it does is allow you to add various controllers that Windows doesn't support very well out of the box, including the DualSense, and Steam is already set up to support DualSense controllers easily. That means you can probably use the controller with most of your games since Steam has a very extensive library of titles, and it will even tell you if a game is optimized to work with the DualSense controller. In fact, even non-Steam games can work this way since you can add your own games to your Steam library. If you haven't yet, you can download Steam here.

If you'd like to configure your DualSense controller, here's how you can get into the necessary settings:

  1. Launch Steam and, from the main window, click Steam and then Settings.
  2. Open the Controller tab on the side menu bar.
  3. By default, Steam already enabled Steam input for PlayStation controllers in games that don't natively support them. If you want Steam's settings to override native support in games that have it, change the PlayStation Controller Support dropdown menu to Enabled.
  4. To test and configure the controller inputs, click Begin Test next to Test Device Inputs.
  5. Here, you can test that the inputs are all recognized correctly. If you want to change them, click Setup Device Inputs and follow the instructions.
  6. Click Back when you're done.
  7. To configure controller inputs as keyboard inputs, scroll down and click Edit next to Desktop layout. Here, you can assign each button to a different key.
  8. If you want to calibrate gyro controls, joystick deadzones, or even change the LED light settings on your controller, you can click Open next to Calibration and advanced settings. This should be optional, though.

That's all you should need to do in order to set up your PlayStation controller. In fact, you shouldn't really need to do anything unless you want to change any settings considering Steam already supports PlayStation controllers out of the box. This is all in case you want to dive deeper into those settings.

Using the DualSense controller with non-Steam games

Now, say you have a game installed from outside of Steam, such as from the Epic Games Store. You can still use this method to configure your PlayStation 5 controller. All you need to do is:

  1. Open Steam
  2. Click Games in the menu at the top, and choose Add a non-Steam game to My Library.
  3. Find the executable file for the game you want to add.

This will let you use the Steam configuration tool with almost any non-Steam game. However, there may be some exceptions, specifically Microsoft Store games. Because these games aren't stored in user-accessible folders, they're not as easy to add to your Steam library.

How to set up the DualSense controller with DS4Windows

Another option to get your PS5 (or PS4) controller working on your Windows PC without using Steam is to use an app called DS4Windows. This is specifically designed to make third-party controllers, such as the DualShock 4 and DualSense, be recognized by the computer as Xbox 360 controllers (which are fundamentally the same as the latest Xbox controllers).

Setting things up is fairly easy. You simply need to download DS4Windows from GitHub and install it. The program will install some required drivers to make your PS5 controller recognized as an Xbox controller, which may require a reboot. But once you connect your controller again, you'll be good to go. The controls on your controller will be automatically mapped to the equivalent on Xbox. Still, you can configure different profiles, so the buttons are mapped in the most comfortable way for you.

One thing to note is that if you're trying to use DS4Windows in Steam games, you'll need to disable both PlayStation and Xbox controller configuration support in the Steam settings in order for things to work normally.

Keep in mind that the current release of DS4Windows is also the last one, so it's possible this tool will stop working at some point. For now, though, this is still a good solution.

What Steam games support the DualSense controller?

Thanks to the built-in support for DualSense controllers on Steam, most of the games that support controller can be used with a DualSense controller without too many issues. Thankfully, Valve makes it easy to see what games do support the PlayStation 5 DualSense controller with a running list of supported games, including partial or full support.

Some titles may be designed specifically for the DualSense controller, too, meaning they can use features like haptic feedback and adaptive triggers. There's a running list of these games on PCGamingWiki.

The DualSense is a great controller for PC gaming

Whether you're a bigger fan of the PlayStation controller designs or you simply want to use a controller you already have, this is a great way to get a similar experience to what Xbox controllers usually offer on Windows, and it can be a much better control method for some games.

One thing you may need to look out for is that since the PlayStation 5 controller has a speaker, it can sometimes become the default audio output for Windows when you connect it, which means the audio experience won't be great. You can use the Quick Actions panel in Windows 11 to choose a different default audio output.

If you're looking to play the latest and greatest games on your PC, you may also want some new hardware that can handle them. Check out our list of the best gaming laptops if you want to buy a new one.

  • PlayStation DualSense Wireless Controller

    The official PlayStation 5 controller comes with the standard button and analog stick layout, but now featuring advanced haptics and adaptive triggers.

    $69 at Amazon $70 at Best Buy
  • PlayStation DualSense Edge Wireless Controller
    $200 at Best Buy $199 at Amazon
item.description:
Quick Links

  • Connecting the PS5 controller to your PC
  • How to set up the DualSense controller with Steam
  • Using the DualSense controller with non-Steam games
  • What Steam games support the DualSense controller?

When you think of PC gaming, you probably think of a mouse and keyboard. That makes sense considering you already use a mouse and keyboard for everything else and they're incredibly versatile and precise. But some games are just best played with a controller, and if you happen to own a PlayStation 5, you can use the DualSense controller with your Windows 11 (or 10) PC.

The DualSense has a significantly different design from its predecessors. The Xbox Wireless Controller is still arguably the best PC controller on the market, but the DualSense is certainly good enough that if you have a PlayStation 5 already, you can avoid buying a new controller and just stick with the controller that came with your console. Here's how you can set it up.

Related
How to optimize Windows 11 for gaming

PC gamers on Windows 11, take note

Connecting the PS5 controller to your PC

Wired connection

There are two ways to connect the DualSense controller to your PC. The easiest is to simply plug in using the USB Type-C port on the controller and a USB port on your PC. You just need a USB cable, which you probably already have, whether it's from your phone or another device. The cable does need to support data throughput, which some cables don't so if you try one and it doesn't work, try a different cable before you give up.

Bluetooth (wireless) connection

If you want to go wireless, the PlayStation 5 controller also supports Bluetooth so that you can connect that way. Most modern laptops and PCs have Bluetooth these days, though you may need an adapter for older machines. Connecting a controller this way is still fairly straightforward. Here's how it goes:

  1. Open the Settings app on your PC and go to the Bluetooth & devices section (in Windows 11) or Devices (in Windows 10).
  2. Click Add device at the top of the page.
  3. Choose Bluetooth in the window that pops up to start searching for devices.
  4. Enter pairing mode on the PlayStation 5 controller by holding the PlayStation and Share buttons at the same time until the lights around the touchpad turn on.
  5. On your PC, the controller should appear as a Wireless controller. Select it and wait for the connection to finish.

Now your controller is connected to your PC, but the experience isn't exactly seamless from here. Windows is designed to specifically work with one kind of input based on the Xbox Wireless Controller, so that's what most games support out of the box. Thankfully, there are solutions for this.

  4. To test and configure the controller inputs, click Begin Test next to Test Device Inputs.
  5. Here, you can test that the inputs are all recognized correctly. If you want to change them, click Setup Device Inputs and follow the instructions.
  6. Click Back when you're done.
  7. To configure controller inputs as keyboard inputs, scroll down and click Edit next to Desktop layout. Here, you can assign each button to a different key.
  8. If you want to calibrate gyro controls, joystick deadzones, or even change the LED light settings on your controller, you can click Open next to Calibration and advanced settings. This should be optional, though.

That's all you should need to do in order to set up your PlayStation controller. In fact, you shouldn't really need to do anything unless you want to change any settings considering Steam already supports PlayStation controllers out of the box. This is all in case you want to dive deeper into those settings.

Now, say you have a game installed from outside of Steam, such as from the Epic Games Store. You can still use this method to configure your PlayStation 5 controller. All you need to do is:

  1. Open Steam
  2. Click Games in the menu at the top, and choose Add a non-Steam game to My Library.
  3. Find the executable file for the game you want to add.

This will let you use the Steam configuration tool with almost any non-Steam game. However, there may be some exceptions, specifically Microsoft Store games. Because these games aren't stored in user-accessible folders, they're not as easy to add to your Steam library.

But once you connect your controller again, you'll be good to go. The controls on your controller will be automatically mapped to the equivalent on Xbox. Still, you can configure different profiles, so the buttons are mapped in the most comfortable way for you.

One thing to note is that if you're trying to use DS4Windows in Steam games, you'll need to disable both PlayStation and Xbox controller configuration support in the Steam settings in order for things to work normally.

Keep in mind that the current release of DS4Windows is also the last one, so it's possible this tool will stop working at some point. For now, though, this is still a good solution.

You can use the Quick Actions panel in Windows 11 to choose a different default audio output.

    The official PlayStation 5 controller comes with the standard button and analog stick layout, but now featuring advanced haptics and adaptive triggers.

    $69 at Amazon $70 at Best Buy
  • PlayStation DualSense Edge Wireless Controller
    $200 at Best Buy $199 at Amazon
